title: Why two paragraphs will not sit side by side
testCase: Is the price on one line, with the amount in <strong>?
blocks.0.text: You are selling your bike. You wrote the price as two paragraphs, because that was what you had at hand. Run it and look.
blocks.1.text: They sit one below the other. But you want it on one line: "Price: 25 leva".
blocks.3.text: Delete the Enter between the two paragraphs and glue them next to each other. Run. They split again. Put a space between them. Same. Write them on one line — nothing changes.
blocks.4.text: You already learned the browser ignores blank lines. This is different: even with zero blank lines, the two paragraphs still stay apart. So the split does not come from your typing. It comes from the tag itself.
blocks.5.text: There are two kinds of tags
blocks.6.items.0:
Block — each takes a whole line and pushes the next thing down, however short it is:

,

…
blocks.6.items.1: Inline — they live inside the line, between the words, and do not break it: <strong>, <em>, <a>
blocks.7.text: A block tag is a line. An inline tag is a word.
blocks.8.text: So the fix is not to nudge the two paragraphs around — two blocks will never stand side by side. Merge them into ONE paragraph, and inside it mark only the part that should stand out:
blocks.10.text: The line stays whole. <strong> does not break it — it only makes the words inside it bold. Why it is called "strong" and not "bold", and why it is NOT a tag for looks — in the next lesson.
blocks.12.text: Your turn. Make the price show on one line, with the amount in bold.
